<findings>

[suggestion] [critical] Zone "Zone Detection Engine" (packages-sourcevision:zone-detection-engine) has catastrophic risk (score: 0.71, cohesion: 0.29, coupling: 0.71) — requires immediate architectural intervention

</findings>

<insights>

- Low cohesion (0.29) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
- High coupling (0.71) — 11 imports target "analysis-engine"
- Louvain, zone-hash, and zones.ts form a coherent clustering subsystem, but the low cohesion score suggests one or more files have import patterns that stray outside this subsystem.
- Three focused test files (zone-detection, zone-size-policy, zone-subdivision) provide good behavioral coverage of the core clustering decisions.
- High coupling to surrounding zones means changes to the zone detection algorithm are likely to ripple across the codebase — stabilizing the public API surface of zones.ts would reduce this risk.
- Cohesion of 0.29 is below the 0.4 threshold, indicating that the files grouped here have sparse internal import connections relative to their external ones — zone-hash.ts in particular may be imported more by consumers than by its zone peers.
- Coupling of 0.71 exceeds the 0.6 warning threshold; combined with bidirectional imports to both the Analysis Engine and Branch Work Analysis zones, the zone detection subsystem is a high-traffic hub that could become a bottleneck for future refactors.
- The Louvain algorithm (louvain.ts) and the zone lifecycle manager (zones.ts) are co-located but serve distinct concerns — algorithm versus orchestration; splitting them into separate modules would improve testability and lower coupling.
- With only 6 files and 0.71 coupling, this zone has the worst cohesion-to-size ratio in the codebase — small zones with high coupling are the highest refactoring risk because every file change has a disproportionate cross-zone blast radius.
- The three test files (zone-detection, zone-size-policy, zone-subdivision) cover distinct behavioral concerns that map to separate production responsibilities — this test structure implicitly confirms the zone should be split, as each test file likely corresponds to a separable module.
- Zone-detection-engine has the worst cohesion/coupling ratio (0.29/0.71) at the smallest meaningful size (6 files) — this combination makes it the highest per-file refactoring risk in the codebase and the most urgent candidate for boundary stabilization.
- The 1:1 ratio of test files to production files (3 test files for louvain.ts, zone-hash.ts, zones.ts) provides unusually thorough coverage but also implies each test file couples tightly to a single production file — if the zone is split, the test-to-production mapping will remain clean, which removes one common obstacle to splitting.
- zone-hash.ts produces stable deterministic IDs for zones across analysis runs; if output-generation modules (llms.txt, CONTEXT.md writers) consume zone IDs they must also consume zone-hash.ts, making it a shared utility that has been accidentally enclosed inside the detection zone rather than placed in a shared foundation.
- zone-hash.ts generates stable zone IDs consumed by both zone detection and output generation phases; enclosing it inside the zone-detection-engine zone forces output writers to import across a zone boundary into the detection subsystem rather than from a shared utility layer — extract to a dedicated utility zone.
- louvain.ts is the only file in the entire src/analyzers/ directory named after a specific algorithm rather than a functional responsibility (compare: zones.ts, zone-hash.ts, imports.ts, inventory.ts). If the Louvain algorithm is replaced with another community detection algorithm, the filename becomes a stale implementation detail leak — rename to graph-clustering.ts or community-detection.ts to match the functional naming convention used by all peer files.
- The three test files (zone-detection.test.ts, zone-size-policy.test.ts, zone-subdivision.test.ts) cover zones.ts behaviors but there is no dedicated louvain.test.ts — this means the Louvain algorithm is tested only indirectly through zones.ts integration, not in isolation. If the clustering algorithm is replaced or tuned, there is no unit-level regression harness to validate the change.
- The zone name 'zone-detection-engine' uses the suffix '-engine' while its primary file is zones.ts (not zone-detection.ts or zone-detection-engine.ts) — the file name and zone name follow different naming conventions, creating a lookup mismatch for developers navigating from zone metadata to source files.
- Rename louvain.ts to graph-clustering.ts or community-detection.ts to align with the functional naming convention used by all peer files in src/analyzers/ — algorithm names leak implementation details and become stale when the algorithm changes.
- Add a dedicated unit test for the Louvain clustering algorithm in isolation (louvain.test.ts or graph-clustering.test.ts) — the current test suite exercises zones.ts integration but provides no regression harness if the clustering algorithm is swapped or parameterized differently.
- [call graph] 245 internal calls, 589 outgoing, 54 incoming (cohesion: 0.29, coupling: 0.71)

</insights>
